narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it spotted in your profile that echoed too many stories of struggle. At the top of what swayed the model was your LSAT score of 29.5, which stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward potential failure, as it falls on the lower side compared to those who typically power through the bar. Layered on that, your Year 3 decile of 1.0 showed early warning signs of challenges keeping pace with peers, making it the second-strongest factor tipping the scales downward. Your Year 1 decile of 1.0 reinforced this, painting a picture of consistent hurdles right from the start of law school rigor. Even your middle family income bracket played a role, as the model links it to added pressures that have tripped up others in similar spots. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.4, while solid, didn't quite counterbalance these, adding its own subtle nudge toward caution. Being a white female full-time student rounded out the influences, each contributing in ways that, together, built a 68% predicted chance of not passing the bar—it's this combination that made the model hesitate on your application. You're clearly driven, aiming full steam ahead at law, but these academic markers created a narrative the model couldn't ignore. The good news? This isn't set in stone—you can retake the LSAT, build those deciles higher with targeted study, or seek scholarships to ease financial strains. Many have rewritten their stories from here; you've got the grit, and a stronger profile next time could flip the script entirely. Keep pushing—you're closer than it feels.
